data.title: Trump says he ordered Nigeria strike delayed by a day to ‘give a Christmas present’
data.description:
US President ‌Donald Trump said on Friday that a US military strike targeting Islamic State militants in Nigeria was originally supposed to take place on Wednesday but he ordered it delayed by a day.
“They were going to ‍do it earlier,” Trump told Politico in an ‌interview. “And I ⁠said, ‘nope, let’s give a Christmas present’. … They didn’t think that was coming but we hit them hard. ‌Every camp got decimated.”
Thursday’s strikes marked a major escalation in an offensive that the West African country’s overstretched military has struggled with for years.
Trump had said on social media that the “powerful and deadly” strikes in the state of Sokoto were carried out against Islamic State gunmen who were “targeting and viciously killing, primarily, innocent Christians”.
Residents and security analysts have said Nigeria’s security crisis affects both Christians, predominant in the south, and Muslims, who are the majority in the north.
Nigeria, which is battling multiple armed groups, said the US strikes were part of an exchange of intelligence and strategic coordination between the two countries.
Associated Press could not confirm the extent of the strikes’ impact. US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth, in a post on social media about the strikes, wrote: “More to come.”

Sanusi Madabo, a 40-year-old farmer in the Nigerian village of Jabo, was preparing for bed on Thursday night when he heard a loud noise that sounded like a plane crashing. He rushed out of his mud house with his wife to see the sky glowing a bright red.
The light burned bright for hours, Madabo said: “It was almost like daytime.”
He did not learn until later that he had witnessed a US attack on an alleged Islamic State camp.
Residents of Jabo, in the northwestern state of Sokoto, told Associated Press in interviews on Friday that they were seized with panic and confusion at the strikes.
They also said the village had never been attacked by armed gangs as part of the violence the US says is widespread, though such attacks regularly happen in neighbouring villages.
“As it approached our area, the heat became intense,” said Abubakar Sani, who lives just a few houses from the scene of the explosion.
“Our rooms began to shake, and then fire broke out,” he said. “The Nigerian government should take appropriate measures to protect us as citizens. We have never experienced anything like this before.”
The Nigerian military did not respond to an Associated Press request asking how many locations were targeted.

The strikes are the outcome of a months-long tense diplomatic clash between the West African nation and the United States.
The Trump administration has said Nigeria is experiencing a Christian genocide, a claim the Nigerian government has rejected.
But now Nigeria’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said the strikes resulted from intelligence sharing and strategic coordination between the two governments.
Yusuf Tuggar, Nigeria’s foreign minister, called the strikes a “new phase of an old conflict” and said he expected more strikes to follow.
“For us, it is something that has been ongoing,” Tuggar added, referring to attacks that have targeted Christians and Muslims in Nigeria for years.
Bulama Bukarti, a security analyst on sub-Saharan Africa at the Tony Blair Institute, said the fear of residents is compounded by a lack of information.
Residents say there were no casualties and security operatives have cordoned off the area.
But the Nigerian government has not released information about the militants who were targeted or any post-strike assessment of casualties.
“What can help in dousing the tension is for the American and Nigerian governments to declare who was targeted, what was attacked, and what has happened so far,” Bukarti said.
Such information is “still missing, and the more opaque the governments are, the more panic there would be on the ground, and that is what will escalate tension”.

Analysts say the strikes might have been intended for the Lakurawa group, a relatively new entrant to Nigeria’s complex security crisis.
The group’s first attack was recorded around 2018 in the northwestern region before the Nigerian government officially announced its presence last year. The composition of the group has been documented by security researchers as primarily consisting of foreigners from the Sahel region of Africa.
However, experts say ties between the Lakurawa group and Islamic State are unproven. The Islamic State West African Province, a branch of Isis in Nigeria, has its strongholds in the northeastern part of the country, where it is currently involved in a power struggle with its parent organisation, Boko Haram.
“What might have happened is that, working with the American government, Nigeria identified Lakurawa as a threat and identified camps that belong to the group,” Bukarti said.
Meanwhile, some local people feel vulnerable.
Aliyu Garba, a village leader in Jabo, said debris left by the strikes was scattered and residents rushed to the scene. Some picked up pieces of the debris, hoping for valuable metal to trade, and Garba said he fears they could get hurt.
For 17-year-old Balira Sa’idu, the strike rattled her as she prepared to get married.
“I am supposed to be thinking about my wedding but right now I am panicking,” she said. “The strike has changed everything. My family is afraid and I don’t even know if it is safe to continue with the wedding plan in Jabo.”
Reporting by Reuters, Agence France-Presse

data.title: What are the secrets to a long and healthy life? A longevity expert reveals
data.description:
There exist thousands of books that focus on longevity and healthy ageing, including bestsellers like Lifespan: Why We Age – and Why We Don’t Have To by David Sinclair, Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity by Peter Attia, and Ageless: The New Science of Getting Older Without Getting Old by Andrew Steele.
Recent years have also seen a huge rise in lifestyle medicine, consumer health products, longevity retreats, documentaries like The Man Who Wants to Live Forever, and published advice on how to live to 100 and beyond.
Ethel May Caterham, who is the oldest person in the world at 116, lives in a nursing home in Surrey, England. She managed to become a “supercentenarian” – a person aged 110 or older – without any of the above.
Born in 1909, Caterham has lived through both world wars, the Great Depression, world-changing advances in technology and two major pandemics: the Spanish Flu and Covid-19, the latter of which she contracted and survived in 2020 at the age of 110. She has outlived her husband by half a century and both of her daughters.

And she has not just lived a long life. Reports indicate that she remains in good health.
Her secrets to living to such an old age, she says, include never arguing with anybody, enjoying everything in moderation, saying yes to every opportunity – because you never know what it will lead to – and having a positive mental attitude.
Caterham’s long, healthy life is extraordinary when considering the observation of Bill Gifford, a journalist who contributed to Attia’s Outlive. “The human body [and brain] is not designed to age well,” he says.
Once we have reproduced, or passed the age of reproduction, “things start to fall apart”, Gifford says. “And evolution is pretty much fine with that; the evidence suggests that we are built to be 50-year-old grandparents.”
It is all downhill after that as our risk of heart disease and cancer – the two leading killers – begins to rise exponentially, he says.
“The trick is almost to defy evolution in some sense, to combat that decline and maintain physical and cognitive function as we get older,” Gifford says. “It’s tricky, but it can be done – and we’ve all seen examples of that, too.”
Caterham is a case in point.
Gifford and Attia are “obsessed” with centenarians – people who live to 100 and beyond – “not because they’ve reached some magic age, but because of how they get there”.
Getting there means delaying, even dodging, killer diseases like cancer and heart disease while at the same time “maintaining some level of physical and intellectual function for decades longer than the average person”.
“It’s like a time shift, as a result of which they get 15 to 20 more years of healthy life,” Gifford says.

Gifford remembers that his grandmother was still putting on make-up every day and walking up and down stairs until she was nearly 100. The average American, on the other hand, “encounters some chronic disease or disability by around age 66”.
The key, the authors say, is to delay disease and maintain physical function – that is, to keep moving and maintain our cognitive and emotional health for as long as possible.
Gifford says that one thing we are getting wrong about how to age well is “arguing too much about this or that diet, or method of fasting, or what type of exercise everyone is supposed to be doing”.
Let us first consider diet.
“The entire food industry is organised around trying to get you to eat way too many calories,” Gifford says. “So ‘diets’ are simply a way to avoid that, and different strategies work for different people.”
He refers to the landmark Predimed study (Prevencion con Dieta Mediterranea), which began in Spain in the early 2000s. The study investigated the effects of a Mediterranean diet supplemented with either extra virgin olive oil or nuts, compared with a control group eating a low-fat diet.
Those receiving a weekly supply of extra virgin olive oil or nuts had about 30 per cent fewer major cardiovascular events, such as heart attack and stroke, relative to the control group.
“The olive oil and the nuts prompted them to eat better and consume less junk food,” Gifford says.

The Mediterranean diet group also showed improvements in eating habits, including a decrease in junk food consumption. The results are widely cited as transformative evidence for the health benefits of Mediterranean dietary patterns.
Gifford says that beyond food, the real problem is a lack of fitness.
He and Attia found that low fitness, both in terms of strength and cardiovascular capacity, including endurance and how fast you can run or walk, “is a massive, massive risk factor for early death and disability”.
Gifford describes most people as being “shockingly unfit”. Because of this, they are “totally unprepared for the decline that comes with age. Simply going from the bottom quarter of aerobic fitness for your age to the middle cuts your risk of dying in half”.
Aerobic fitness is measured in VO2 max (maximal oxygen uptake), meaning the maximum amount of oxygen your body can use during intense exercise. It is a key measure of cardiovascular endurance and is usually expressed in millilitres of oxygen per kilogram of body weight per minute (ml/kg/min).
The higher your VO2 max, the better your body’s ability to deliver and use oxygen for physical performance and stamina.
Gifford himself focuses on endurance exercise. “I like to go places under my own power – on a bike, on a paddle board or other kind of boat, or on foot.”
But it is not just about aerobic fitness. “It’s the same with strength.”

Simply not being sedentary – not sitting still all day or for many hours a day – and working on your fitness is a “huge health hack”, Gifford says.
“The trick is changing someone’s habits around activity and food.”
Caterham is described as having been an “active” person all her life, driving a car until she was 97, playing bridge regularly after her 100th birthday, and enjoying the garden, which she still does today.
Gifford’s top advice to extend longevity and healthspan
Add some kind of activity to your life, Gifford suggests. Aim for two sessions a week, but one session is better than none. Walking, in particular, is hugely underrated.
“I think the healthiest thing I do all day is walking my dogs in the morning,” he says. “I get a little bit of exercise, I get a bit of sunlight and nature exposure, and I see people I know in the neighbourhood.”
As for breaking bad habits, he says the less time you can spend on social media, the better.
“Your phone is an incredible device for communicating and gathering information, so use it for that and not for wasting time that could be better spent taking care of your physical body or your mind.”
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Academia must ensure AI is used responsibly
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) has been a great boost to productivity in many professional fields, including academia. But it can also be a double-edged sword. Easy access, low costs and convenience have meant that more students are handing in AI-generated assignments without doing their own reading and writing. But misuse has also affected some scholars. Such cases must be addressed, and awareness of responsibility involving AI needs to be raised to uphold academic standards and integrity.
One such incident occurred at the University of Hong Kong recently, which involved one of the city’s leading population and suicide prevention policy experts. Unfortunately, Professor Paul Yip Siu-fai had to step down as an associate dean of the social sciences faculty after agreeing to retract a paper he co-authored with a PhD student in a historical study of fertility in Hong Kong. As the paper’s lead author, the PhD student failed to disclose AI-generated results that referenced non-existent publications.
AI can be a great research tool, but its accuracy and reliability are not guaranteed. Great care and repeated checks are needed, as with the use of any sources during research.
Yip is a leading scholar and government adviser. Hopefully, his important work will not be affected. But let this be a cautionary tale for all about the use of AI. Appropriately, the university is now implementing mandatory training and assessments for all researchers to ensure they use AI-related resources properly.
Universities around the world are integrating AI tools into teaching and research. At the same time, they need to balance such usage so they are not subject to misuse. It is now an uphill battle for professors to discourage students from relying on AI to generate essays and assignments, a practice that defeats the very purpose of academic learning, if not worse. Meanwhile, in more scientific fields, the outcomes of research with AI-assistance have, in some instances, led to questions about their validity. Such a powerful and rapidly evolving technology is bound to change how universities teach and conduct research. It has opened up a brave new world of knowledge and errors for students and professors to navigate.

data.title: Chinese chipmaker CXMT in crosshairs of South Korean prosecutors over Samsung tech leak
data.description:
Prosecutors in South Korea have indicted 10 people, including former Samsung Electronics executives, for allegedly leaking the company’s technology to Chinese chipmaker ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT).
The Seoul Central District Prosecutors’ Office on Tuesday said five people had been arrested, including a former Samsung executive who allegedly recruited key personnel and leaked the company’s proprietary technology to CXMT, according to a report by Yonhap News Agency.
They face charges for violating the country’s Unfair Competition Prevention Act and the Industrial Technology Protection Act, according to the prosecutor’s Information Technology Crime Investigation Department.
Another five individuals, including CXMT employees, were indicted without detention on the same charges, the Yonhap report said.
South Korean prosecutors alleged that CXMT recruited a former Samsung vice-president as a research department head soon after the Chinese chipmaker was established in 2016. CXMT made the executive responsible for poaching key Samsung engineers to bolster development of its 10-nanometre dynamic random access memory (DRAM) technology.
The legal fallout has cast a shadow over CXMT, which had made strides to catch up with global makers of advanced memory chips and pushed forward Beijing’s drive for semiconductor self-reliance.
Samsung and CXMT did not immediately respond to requests for comments on Friday.

A former Samsung researcher also allegedly copied by hand the company’s “Process Recipe Plan” for DRAMs – a critical document of its manufacturing programme – and provided it to CMXT, prosecutors said in a press conference on Tuesday.
The authorities argued that those actions enabled CMXT to become the first Chinese company to successfully mass-produce 10-nm DRAMs in 2023, which resulted in an estimated sales decline of 5 trillion won (US$3.5 billion) for Samsung in 2024 alone.
Last month, CXMT achieved breakthroughs with the launch of its new Double Data Rate 5 (DDR5) DRAM products, reflecting the company’s efforts to challenge the dominance of South Korean memory giants Samsung and SK Hynix and US-based Micron Technology.
With high frequency and expanded memory, CXMT’s new DDR5 products are targeted at state-of-the-art AI computing servers and stacks.
The Chinese chipmaker had also unveiled the LPDDR5X series, an advanced DRAM product line targeted at mobile devices, which entered mass production earlier this year.
CXMT was also preparing an initial public offering on the mainland, after completing in July its counselling recordation, the formal filing of pre-listing guidance with the securities regulator.

data.title: Hong Kong’s re-engineered 2026 census is rightly moving with the times
data.description:
The traditional population census can seem a tedious exercise in form filling and answering questions about various aspects of our lives that comes around only once every five years. That does not do it justice. A census provides governments, businesses and researchers with data on demographics such as age, sex, ethnicity, marital status and children, and socio-economics like income, education and housing.
They use it to guide policy, plan services like schools, transport and hospitals, allocate resources and understand community needs.
The census is therefore a periodically updated snapshot of the community. But it is labour-intensive and does not come cheap, requiring the hiring of 7,000 temporary field staff for the last round. It may not seem an exercise that leaves much room for economising, but that reckons without the modern engineering of data collection and analysis.
This has now enabled the Hong Kong authorities to exempt 90 per cent of households from the five-yearly population census next year and opt for a model that also uses existing data to track the city’s demographics.
In a major break from decades of statistical tradition, Commissioner for Census and Statistics Leo Yu Chun-keung says the 2026 census has been revamped to improve workflow while saving on manpower. The department will use existing data, ranging from immigration entry permits to birth records, to calculate population benchmarks in the 2026 exercise and to replace the “short-form” questionnaire in the full census from 2031.
Detailed inquiries will be limited to a random sample of just 10 per cent of households every five years. Assistant Commissioner Sharon Ng Pun-wai says that for the new model, the department would hire just 200 non-civil-servants, keeping total expenditure at HK$550 million. The 300,000 households selected for the next census must complete a detailed survey.
Traditionalists may have misgivings about abandoning the old door-to-door model involving detailed form filling. But the new model promises more timely and efficient data collection. The problem with the laborious old process is the risk that, by the time collection and assessment is complete, the world will have moved on – particularly in this age of rapid change. So we might never quite get an accurate five-year snapshot.
In that sense, the re-engineering is a positive development, as long as the authorities ensure the data remains sufficiently comprehensive. Indeed, with fast, effective sampling, the data can be used to dive deeper into the city’s demographics and socio-economics.

data.title: The Cure guitarist and keyboard player Perry Bamonte dies aged 65
data.description:
The Cure guitarist and keyboard player Perry Bamonte has died aged 65, the legendary British dark rock band announced on Friday.
Bamonte, who became a full member of The Cure in 1990, “passed away after a short illness at home over Christmas”, according to a statement published on the band’s website.
“It is with enormous sadness that we confirm the death of our great friend and bandmate Perry Bamonte,” it said.
“Quiet, intensive, intuitive, constant and hugely creative, ‘Teddy’ was a warm hearted and vital part of The Cure story.”
The statement added: “Our thoughts and condolences are with all his family, he will be very greatly missed.”
The Cure – whose 66-year-old frontman, Robert Smith, is famous for his innovative approach to music as well as onstage stamina – has transformed itself throughout its prolific decades-spanning existence.
The Cure formed in 1976 and Bamonte was initially part of its road crew from 1984 before enjoying two stints as a musical member, playing guitar, six-string bass and keyboard.
The first, from 1990, spanned 14 years; the second stint was from 2022 onwards.

In the statement, The Cure noted Bamonte had helped create numerous albums including Wish (1992), Wild Mood Swings (1996), Bloodflowers (2000), Acoustic Hits (2001), and The Cure (2004).
He performed in more than 400 shows during his first 14-year period in the band and then played another 90 after rejoining three years ago, it added.
Those later gigs were “some of the best in the band’s history”, the statement said.
Led by Smith’s plaintive, wailing voice and rich guitar textures, The Cure helped shape Goth rock with albums including 1982’s Pornography, which was full of suicidal imagery.
The band later had hits with much cheerier material, including the track “Friday I’m in Love”.
They continued touring but before last year’s release of Songs of a Lost World had not released a new studio album since 2008’s 4:13 Dream.
The band’s former drummer, Lol Tolhurst, said in a tribute posted on Instagram that he was “so sad” to learn of Bamonte’s death, adding that he and his wife “send our condolences to all that knew him”.
“Farewell Teddy,” he added.
Bamonte was also the bass player in supergroup Love Amongst Ruin, which has released two studio albums.

data.title: Meet comedian Morgan Jay, who went viral for his auto-tuned musical acts
data.description:
TikTok-famous comedian and musician Morgan Jay is booked and busy for 2026. He’s taking his Goofy Guy Tour far and wide, from the US to Asia, Australia and New Zealand.
In Asia, shows in Taipei, Hong Kong, Manila, Singapore and Kuala Lumpur have already sold out. So the Los Angeles-based online sensation has added late shows in Hong Kong, Manila and Kuala Lumpur, though the latter two are also fully booked.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Morgan Jay (@morganjay)



Jay is best known for combining music with comedy. His signature bit, where he invites audience members to sing into an auto-tuned microphone during his crowd work segments, often produces hilarious results. He rose to fame after clips of these interactions with fans started going viral on social media. He has nearly 5.5 million followers on Instagram at the time of writing.
So what else do we know about Morgan Jay?
What’s his background?

Born in February 1987, Jay is from New Jersey, and has Brazilian and Italian roots. His mother hails from Brazil, while his Sicilian father, a musician, grew up in Argentina, he revealed in an interview with Ashley Parchment on her YouTube channel, It Takes a Universe. He lost his dad at a young age to cancer, after which his mother took up multiple jobs to make ends meet. Jay started playing piano at a young age, following in the footsteps of his older brother. However, he did not enjoy it.
He’s an actor

In high school, Jay was involved in choir and theatre, he shared on It Takes a Universe. He went on to participate in a drama programme at the New York Film Academy, and studied theatre at the New York University Tisch School of the Arts alongside Miles Teller and Ilana Glazer. You may have seen Jay in shows like Night Court, St Denis Medical, Girls5eva and the 2025 film Cotton Candy Bubble Gum.
How did he get into comedy?












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Morgan Jay (@morganjay)



Jay was a self-professed class clown at school, he told Ashley Parchment. He started doing stand-up comedy during his time at NYU. After graduating, he interned for Conan O’Brien during his stint on The Tonight Show, which taught him “how to be a ruthless editor of my own material”, he told Variety.
Jay spent the first five years of his comedy career in New York before moving to Los Angeles. His shows took off when he ventured into musical comedy. During the pandemic, he came up with his unique auto-tuned crowd work segments and blew up on social media.

data.title: Wild goose chase? France works to rouse China’s appetite for pricey, imported foie gras
data.description:
From luxury handbags to vintage Bordeaux, China’s consumers have long associated French products with fine living. Now, France aims to hook the country’s expanding middle class on another local speciality: foie gras.
China placed tight restrictions on imports of the iconic French goose liver pate amid Europe’s bird flu epidemic, but there are signs that things could change, with Beijing recently vowing to get more produce “from French farms to Chinese tables”.
Paris and Beijing also signed a declaration on agriculture during French President Emmanuel Macron’s visit to China earlier this month, raising hopes among French exporters that they can break into the country’s vast market.
With French foie gras also facing import bans in other key markets, some producers view China as a potential answer to the industry’s problems. But exporters may be underestimating the challenges they would face in the Chinese market – especially the intense local competition, insiders cautioned.
Still, the industry’s ambitions were on full display at the China International Import Expo – China’s biggest import fair – in Shanghai last month, where the French foie gras industry association CIFOG made its debut appearance.
CIFOG was out to make a splash, with a booth nestled beneath a hulking replica of the Eiffel Tower. Chefs from the Michelin-starred restaurant Maison Lameloise Shanghai were brought in to prepare fresh foie gras, which attracted a crowd – including a visiting French trade minister.
“We really believe in this market,” said Marie-Pierre Pe, director of CIFOG. “The very jet-set Chinese population is similar to the French population, so for us, it’s almost like an El Dorado.”




China already has a sizeable appetite for pate. Though no official data on China’s foie gras consumption is available, state news agency Xinhua reported in November that Linqu county in the eastern province of Shandong – said to account for 70 per cent of the national market – had produced 5,000 tonnes of foie gras that year.
In comparison, France – the world’s biggest consumer of foie gras – devoured 7,275 tonnes of the delicacy in 2023, according to data published by the country’s agriculture ministry this year.
France’s producers believe they can carve out a slice of that market, with CIFOG’s Pe pointing to the dynamic French restaurant scene that has emerged in China’s major cities.
In this year’s Black Pearl Restaurant Guide, widely regarded as China’s answer to the Michelin Guide, 17 French restaurants received one or more diamonds – the most of any non-Chinese cuisine. Even with Chinese cuisines included, French eateries ranked as high as third, behind only Cantonese and Chaozhou (also known as Chiu Chow) restaurants.
“Given the number of visits and tastings at the booth, we are seeing strong interest in French foie gras,” Pe said, adding that CIFOG’s meetings with customs officials and journalists in Shanghai had reinforced their confidence in securing a foothold in the Chinese market.
The industry is under pressure to find new sources of demand. In 2023, France exported 2,825 tonnes of foie gras, just over one-third of its production according to official data. The search for additional markets has been made more acute by an ongoing bird flu epidemic, which Pe said has shut down most shipments to Japan and the United States, both major buyers.


However, China may not prove to be the gold mine producers are looking for. The country is also wary of importing avian products from Europe amid the epidemic.
China imported no fresh or frozen foie gras during the first 10 months of 2025, according to Beijing’s customs data. Pe said most French producers were still banned from exporting to China.
Though Beijing and Paris had signed agreements allowing China to target its customs measures more precisely – blocking imports from a specific region with recorded cases of bird flu – Pe said in practice Beijing still tended to impose bans on the entirety of France even if cases were only appearing in some areas.
And even if Beijing relaxes its restrictions, French foie gras exporters will still have to overcome another formidable challenge in China: the country’s huge domestic industry, which can produce at much lower costs and has a better understanding of local consumption habits, according to industry insiders.
For many Chinese tourists, eating foie gras is one of the highlights of a trip to France, alongside tasting escargots, crepes and macarons. “How can one miss a foie gras feast when in Paris?” read one viral post on the Chinese social platform RedNote. Another user said she had flown to Paris just for a taste of authentic foie gras.
But that enthusiasm may not last after they return home. Jie Zhu, the founder of European gourmet food importer Shanghai Brightview, said individual consumer demand for foie gras remains “negligible” in China. While the product is widely used in Chinese restaurants, he said, “they use very cheap domestic foie gras”.


While French restaurants readily source imported ingredients – Zhu’s customers include Michelin-starred outlets in Shanghai – most consumers in China are Chinese eateries, he said. He added those establishments mainly use foie gras as a flavour enhancer, incorporating the pate into dishes ranging from fried rice to steamed soup, and feel the local version does the job just fine.
China has been making foie gras domestically since 1988, when Shandong’s Linqu county imported a herd of French Landes geese. The county has since built an entire foie gras industrial chain, one which Xinhua reported accounts for 20 per cent of the world’s supply.
Linqu’s producers are now able to churn out foie gras that costs as little as one-quarter the price charged by their French counterparts, which Zhu said made it “unimaginable” for Chinese restaurants to switch.
In 2014, the leading French producer Euralis tried setting up its own Chinese production base, opening a factory in Lianyungang, a port just to the south of Shandong province. But the facility has since ceased production. The firm declined to comment on the issue when contacted by the Post.
Today, the outlook for French foie gras makers looks even tougher as China grapples with an ongoing “consumption downgrade”, which has seen consumers look to cut back on spending amid an economic slowdown, particularly for products seen as luxuries.
The trend has hit China’s dining sector hard, with the Hongcan Industry Research Institute saying in a report that restaurants faced “unprecedented challenges” amid a perfect storm of sluggish demand, falling prices and rising labour and rent costs.
Chinese consumers spent 79.2 yuan (US$11.25) on average per restaurant visit in 2024, a fall of nearly 10 per cent compared with the previous year, according to the report, which added that high-end outlets were struggling badly and losing customers.
If you only do French foie gras, you won’t survive
Jie Zhu, gourmet food importer
“The only thing that owners look at is the price,” Zhu said. “They don’t really care about the quality – they probably can’t tell the difference anyway.”
That said, Zhu said he would definitely start importing French foie gras if China relaxed its import ban, as he expected there would be demand from his clients in fine dining.
But the market size would be limited, he admitted, with the immediate future for restaurants looking “very bad”. Their situation probably would not improve until the second half of next year, he said.
“If you only do French foie gras, you won’t survive.”
Even Chinese producers are feeling the impact of the downturn. Chunguan Food – the biggest foie gras maker in Linqu county, capable of an output of 3,000 tonnes per year – had seen its profit per goose fall from 100 yuan to 50 yuan over the past few years, the company’s general manager, Ma Lijun, told Xinhua.
While French brands try to find a foothold in the Chinese market, China’s companies appear to be headed the other way. Ma said Chunguan Food had plans for a 50 million yuan investment to build an export-grade production line, as the company searches for new market opportunities overseas.
Success abroad would be a breakthrough. During the first 10 months of 2025, mainland China only sent 10 tonnes of fresh foie gras offshore, with 7 tonnes going to Hong Kong and 3 tonnes going to Macau, according to customs data from Beijing.

data.title: Police arrest suspect after man stabs 3 women in separate attacks on Paris Metro
data.description:
French police on Friday arrested a man suspected of stabbing three women in the Paris Metro as the capital’s end-of-year festivities were in full swing, prosecutors said.
The three victims were attacked at three different locations along the Line 3 Metro track that runs across central Paris, the RATP authority that runs the transit service said.
An Agence France-Presse journalist at the Republique station saw a security team treating a woman who had been wounded in the leg and appeared to be in a state of shock.
The attacks happened between 4.15pm and 4.45pm local time at the stations Republique and Arts et Metiers – both next to the Marais district – and the Opera station, the RATP said.
“The victims were quickly taken care of by the emergency services,” it said.
Paris police said two of the women attacked were treated by the emergency services and taken to hospital but they were not in critical condition. A third woman turned up at hospital seeking treatment, they said.
Police used surveillance-camera footage and mobile-tracking tools to locate the 25-year-old suspected attacker in the Val d’Oise region north of Paris, said prosecutors.
“Activating the geolocation of his mobile phone led to his arrest late afternoon in Val d’Oise,” they said.
“The police are on site. Back-up security teams have been deployed to reinforce safety on the line,” it added.


Paris police chief Patrice Faure paid tribute to “the reactivity and the mobilisation” of the investigators that led to the arrest of the suspect. Police arrested him at 6.55pm, less than three hours after the first attack, he said.
Interior Minister Laurent Nunez congratulated the different police services involved in tracking down the suspect.
Transport police have opened an investigation into attempted murder and assault with a weapon.
European capitals are especially vigilant during the end-of-year period for any violent incidents, given recent attacks and plots targeting festive or religious gatherings.
Last week, Nunez called for “maximum vigilance” in a message to senior officials.
Due to the “very high level of the terrorist threat” and “the risk of public disorder”, Nunez asked local officials to strengthen security measures across the country with a visible and deterrent presence.
Nunez specifically asked for particular attention to be paid to security on public transport.

data.title: 10 most popular diamond cuts, from round brilliant to triangle – but which is Taylor Swift rocking?
data.description:
Nothing stirs up excitement quite like celebrity jewellery sightings, and this year there have been plenty of dazzling diamond debuts, from Zendaya to Selena Gomez and her long-time BFF Taylor Swift.
Swift practically launched a trend for antique-inspired sparklers when she and American football star Travis Kelce announced their engagement in August. He proposed to the pop megastar with an old-mine-cut diamond, a vintage-style cushion stone characterised by a squarish outline, rounded corners and soft, romantic sparkle, with Kindred Lubeck of Artifex Fine Jewelry designing the ring.

Back in May, Kim Kardashian made a bold statement with the shape and cut of her jewels when she arrived at the Palais de Justice courtroom in Paris to testify at the trial of those accused of stealing her jewellery in 2016. The billionaire mogul wore a smattering of gems, including a spectacular diamond necklace featuring a 10.13-carat pear-shaped centre stone as well as an ear cuff adorned with 12 pear-shaped diamonds, which according to some, represent individuality, joy and strength. Sending a message perhaps?
In case you are looking to make your own investment in a signature piece of jewellery, here are 10 of the most popular diamond cuts you should know.
Round brilliant cut

A round-cut diamond is a classic choice that goes beyond seasons and trends, and most round diamonds are brilliant cut. This specific faceting style enhances a diamond’s light reflection and overall shine.
Marquise cut

Also known as the navette cut, meaning “little boat” in French, the marquise cut was supposedly fashioned to resemble the lips of the Marquise de Pompadour, King Louis XV of France’s mistress. Some believe that it symbolises wealth and sophistication.
Oval cut

This cut is often associated with fertility and new beginnings, since the word “oval” originates from the Latin word ovum, meaning “egg”. Modern A-listers such as Lady Gaga, Hailey Bieber and Kourtney Kardashian were all presented with flashy, oval-cut engagement rings.
Pear cut

Shaped like a teardrop, pear-cut diamonds generally appear larger than other cuts of the same weight and are flattering for the hands – particularly for those with shorter or wider fingers. It is said to be tied to feelings of happiness, empowerment and self-expression.
Heart cut

For obvious reasons, the heart shape is considered to be the most charming and feminine of all diamond cuts – a popular style for engagements and anniversaries. It is also one of the most difficult to craft, given that it has symmetrical halves and a central cleft.
Emerald cut

The emerald cut, as the name implies, was originally developed to prevent emeralds from unexpected breakage or chipping. Elegant and understated, the design is marked by an elongated rectangular shape, with distinctive step-cut facets that create a hall-of-mirrors effect.
Baguette cut

This slim, rectangular cut is named after the word baguette, French for “wand” or “rod”. Made famous during the art deco period, the style features pointed edges and is typically used as an accent stone alongside other diamonds.
Triangle cut

Most commonly known as a trillion or trilliant cut, a triangle-cut diamond is a great choice for bold dressers and fashion-forward individuals. It has pointed corners and equal sides that are said to represent the past, present and future of a couple’s journey.
Princess cut

A princess-cut diamond is recognised for its face-up appearance, sharp square outline and brilliant-style facet arrangement. It’s perfectly suited to those looking for something modern with some serious sparkle.
Cushion cut

The cushion cut references the diamond’s pillow-inspired silhouette and slightly curved appearance. Developed in the 18th century, this vintage-inspired design boasts chunkier facets, which create a fiery appearance and broad flashes of light.
